comment: Install-time detonation signatures of the June-2026 Miasma / Mini Shai-Hulud worm as they surface in the process tree under a watched agent: Phantom-Gyp build-time exec, the .github/setup.js dropper / bun-run install hooks, the pinned Bun runtime fetch, the CI runner-memory secret scrape, and the worm's literal IOC marker strings. Network C2 domains live as net-010 in network-exfil.json.

rules.name: Phantom Gyp build-time code execution
rules.description: A node-gyp binding.gyp command-substitution that runs a top-level index.js/setup.js at native-build time. This is the 'Phantom Gyp' evasion (Miasma v2): the package ships NO preinstall/postinstall script, so postinstall scanners see nothing, and instead detonates inside `npm install` when node-gyp shells out the gyp `<!(...)` expression.
rules.guidance:
1. Do not run the build. Inspect the package's binding.gyp 'sources' for a `<!(node ...)` that runs index.js/setup.js.
2. Pin the dependency to a known-good version and reinstall from a clean lockfile.
3. If it already ran, assume credential theft — rotate npm/cloud/GitHub tokens.

rules.name: agent-config dropper / bun-run install hook
rules.description: Execution of the Miasma dropper `node .github/setup.js` (or a `bun run index.js` install hook). The dropper is a multi-megabyte obfuscated script that decrypts a credential-stealer payload and runs it under Bun. It is launched by a planted .claude/.gemini/.cursor/.vscode config when a repo is opened in an agent/IDE, or by a package's preinstall hook.
rules.guidance:
1. Treat the repository as hostile. Do not open it again in an agent/IDE.
2. Inspect .github/setup.js (expect a huge single-line obfuscated blob) and the planted .claude/settings.json / .vscode/tasks.json trigger.
3. If it ran, rotate every credential on the host and check your GitHub account for a new public 'Miasma' repo.

rules.name: pinned Bun runtime fetch (Miasma payload loader)
rules.description: Downloading the specific Bun runtime version (v1.3.13) the Miasma worm pins to execute its decrypted payload. A package install or repo-open step fetching this exact Bun build is the worm's runtime-bootstrap signature.
rules.guidance:
1. A dependency install fetching a Bun runtime is not normal — stop the install.
2. Check %TEMP% (or /tmp) for a downloaded bun/bun.exe and a transient p<random>.js payload.
3. If present, isolate the host and rotate credentials.

rules.name: CI runner-memory secret scrape
rules.description: Reading a GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process memory (/proc/<pid>/mem) to recover masked CI/CD secrets in plaintext. Masking only hides secrets in logs — they remain in process memory, and this technique scrapes GITHUB_TOKEN, cloud keys, and signing tokens directly.
rules.guidance:
1. This is an active CI/CD credential theft. Treat all secrets exposed to the workflow as compromised and rotate them immediately (GITHUB_TOKEN is auto-scoped, but rotate cloud keys, signing tokens, package tokens).
2. Review the workflow run for unexpected steps and pin all actions to full commit SHAs.

rules.name: Miasma / Shai-Hulud IOC marker string
rules.description: A literal indicator-of-compromise string baked into the Miasma / Shai-Hulud worm payloads and dead-drop infrastructure. Any appearance in a command line means the worm's code is present on the host.
rules.guidance: Treat the host as compromised. Rotate ALL credentials (npm, PyPI, GitHub, AWS/Azure/GCP, SSH, GPG), but do NOT simply invalidate the GitHub token in place — the malware has a dead-man switch keyed on token invalidation; revoke and reissue via the web console. Then reinstall from clean, pinned lockfiles.
rules.pattern: Miasma:?\s*The Spreading Blight|IfYouInvalidateThisTokenItWillNukeTheComputerOfTheOwner|DontRevokeOrItGoesBoom|TheBeautifulSandsOfTime|thebeautifulmarchoftime|firedalazer|Shai-?Hulud:?\s*(?:The Second Coming|Here We Go Again)
